edges.description: Activated contractile myofibroblasts populate proliferative palmar nodules.

edges.target: Digital Flexion Contracture and Hand Dysfunction

edges.description: Fibroproliferative collagen deposition can manifest as knuckle pads.
edges.source: Excessive Collagen Deposition and Cord Formation

edges.description: Digital flexion contracture and hand dysfunction are the defining clinical Dupuytren contracture phenotype.
edges.source: Digital Flexion Contracture and Hand Dysfunction

edges.description: Collagen cords tether the fingers in fixed flexion.
edges.source: Digital Flexion Contracture and Hand Dysfunction

edges.description: Fixed digital flexion impairs extension, grip, and hand function.
edges.source: Digital Flexion Contracture and Hand Dysfunction

nodes.description: Mature collagen cords spanning the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints shorten and tether the fingers in fixed flexion, impairing extension, grip, and hand function. The deformity is progressive and, once established, does not spontaneously resolve.
nodes.id: Digital Flexion Contracture and Hand Dysfunction

evidence.reference_title: Scientific understanding and clinical management of Dupuytren disease.
evidence.snippet: Dupuytren disease (DD) is a fibroproliferative disorder of unknown etiology that often results in shortening and thickening of the palmar fascia, leading to permanent and irreversible flexion contracture of the digits.

nodes.description: Progressive flexion contracture of the fingers caused by fibrotic thickening of the palmar fascia; the defining clinical feature of the disease.

evidence.snippet: Dupuytren's disease is a benign fibromatosis of the hands and fingers that leads to flexion contractures.

nodes.description: Activated myofibroblasts deposit excessive extracellular matrix with a characteristic increase in type III collagen relative to type I. As cellular nodules mature into relatively acellular collagen-rich cords, the contracted matrix becomes a fixed structural deformity along the digital rays.
nodes.id: Excessive Collagen Deposition and Cord Formation

evidence.reference_title: Dupuytren's Contracture: A Review of the Literature.
evidence.snippet: The underlying mechanisms involve complex cellular processes, particularly the role of transforming growth factor-beta in promoting fibroblast activity and collagen buildup.

nodes.description: Repetitive microtrauma, local ischemia, and oxidative stress in the palmar aponeurosis initiate a dysregulated wound-healing response. Free radical generation and hypoxia in the palmar fascia are thought to trigger the fibroproliferative cascade rather than restoring normal tissue.
nodes.id: Fascial Microinjury and Aberrant Wound Healing

evidence.reference_title: Scientific understanding and clinical management of Dupuytren disease.
evidence.snippet: DD has been variously attributed to the presence of oxygen free radicals, trauma to the palmar fascia, or aberrant immune responses with altered antigen presentation, or to interactions between these proposed mechanisms.

nodes.description: Fixed flexion of the digits, most commonly the ring (4th) and little (5th) fingers, due to contracture of the digital cords.

evidence.reference_title: Dupuytren's Contracture: A Review of the Literature.
evidence.snippet: Dupuytren's contracture is a chronic condition that affects the palmar fascia, leading to progressive flexion of the fingers, particularly the ring and little fingers.

nodes.description: Loss of finger extension and weakened grip impair activities requiring a flat hand or full grasp.
Fibrous thickenings over the dorsal proximal interphalangeal joints (Garrod nodes/pads), associated with Dupuytren diathesis.

Palmar fibroblasts transdifferentiate into alpha-smooth-muscle-actin-positive myofibroblasts under the influence of TGF-beta. These contractile cells populate the proliferative nodules and generate the active contractile force that, together with collagen deposition, draws the digits into flexion.

evidence.reference_title: Scientific understanding and clinical management of Dupuytren disease.
evidence.snippet: Mechanically, digital contracture is caused by myofibroblasts in the DD palmar fascia; however, the exact origin of this cell type remains unknown.

evidence.reference_title: Dupuytren's Contracture: A Review of the Literature.
evidence.snippet: The underlying mechanisms involve complex cellular processes, particularly the role of transforming growth factor-beta in promoting fibroblast activity and collagen buildup.

nodes.description: Firm subcutaneous nodules in the palm, often the earliest clinical sign, representing myofibroblast-rich proliferative lesions.

Locally produced TGF-beta and other growth factors, together with immune cell infiltration, amplify the fibrotic response and drive resident fibroblasts toward an activated, contractile phenotype. Aberrant Wnt/beta-catenin signaling is a hallmark molecular feature of Dupuytren tissue, and genome-wide association studies additionally implicate developmental Hedgehog and Notch signaling.

evidence.snippet: The fact that six of these nine loci harbor genes encoding proteins in the Wnt-signaling pathway suggests that aberrations in this pathway are key to the process of fibromatosis in Dupuytren's disease.

evidence.reference_title: A genome-wide association meta-analysis implicates Hedgehog and Notch signaling in Dupuytren's disease.
evidence.snippet: Gene prioritization implicated the Hedgehog and Notch signaling pathways.

evidence.reference_title: Scientific understanding and clinical management of Dupuytren disease.
evidence.snippet: The presence of immune cells and related phenomena in DD-affected tissue suggests that DD is possibly immune-related.
